stem: A curve is given parametrically by \( x=[[a]]t+[[b]] \), \( y=[[c]]t+[[d]] \). Eliminate the parameter \( t \) to write the Cartesian equation as \( y=mx+k \). Find \( m \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio, using \( a/c\) instead of \( c/a\).
This mistakenly adds the intercept term \( b\) into the slope.
This flips the sign of the correct slope.
This uses the ratio of the constant shifts \( d/b\) instead of the ratio of the coefficients of \( t\).
This is off by one from the correct slope.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct slope.

stem: A curve is given parametrically by \( x=[[a]]t+[[b]] \), \( y=[[c]]t^{2}+[[d]] \). Eliminate the parameter \( t \) to express \( y \) as a function of \( x \), then find \( y \) when \( x=[[x0]] \).
solution: From \( x=[[a]]t+[[b]] \), \( t=\dfrac{x-[[b]]}{[[a]]} \). Substituting into \( y=[[c]]t^{2}+[[d]] \) gives \( y=[[c]]\left(\dfrac{x-[[b]]}{[[a]]}\right)^{2}+[[d]] \); at \( x=[[x0]] \) this is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to square the substituted expression for \( t\).
This uses the wrong sign when solving \( x=[[a]]t+[[b]]\) for \( t\).
This drops the shift \( b\) entirely when solving for \( t\).
This subtracts the constant \( d\) instead of adding it.
This is off by one from the correct value of \( y\).
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct value of \( y\).

stem: A curve is given parametrically by \( x=[[a]]\cos t \), \( y=[[b]]\sin t \). Eliminate the parameter \( t \) using the Pythagorean identity to find the Cartesian relation between \( x \) and \( y \), then find \( y^{2} \) when \( x=[[x0]] \).
solution: Since \( \cos t=\dfrac{x}{[[a]]} \) and \( \sin t=\dfrac{y}{[[b]]} \), the identity \( \cos^{2}t+\sin^{2}t=1 \) eliminates \( t \) to give \( \dfrac{x^{2}}{[[a]]^{2}}+\dfrac{y^{2}}{[[b]]^{2}}=1 \), i.e. \( y^{2}=[[b]]^{2}\left(1-\dfrac{x^{2}}{[[a]]^{2}}\right) \); at \( x=[[x0]] \) this is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign inside the parentheses, adding the squared ratio instead of subtracting it.
This swaps the roles of \( a\) and \( b\) in the eliminated relation.
This forgets to scale \( x_0^2\) by \( 1/a^2\) before subtracting it from \( 1\).
This drops the leading \( 1\) entirely, keeping only the scaled squared term.
This is off by one from the correct value of \( y^{2}\).
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct value of \( y^{2}\).
